content.full_text:
A-76-07-017


July 27, 1976


Cathryn L. Barr
10292 Dixby Avenue
Garden Grove, California 92640

Dear Ms. Barr:

This letter is to respond to your request of July 10, 1976,
for advice regarding the campaign disclosure requirements of the
Political Reform Act.

The term "contribution" is defined in statute and regulation
to include certain payments for which full and adequate consideration
is not made to the donor, Government Code Section 82015, 2
Cal. Adm. Code Section 18215. On the basis of the information
you stated, including the fact that the loan is payable over a 63
month period and at 3 percent interest, the staff believes that
she lender is receiving full and adequate consideration for the
loan and, consequently, has not made a contribution to you.
She, therefore, is not a major donor committee and is not required
to file a campaign disclosure statement reporting the loan to you.
You, as the recipient of a loan, however, are required to report
receipt of the loan on Schedule B, part 1 of your campaign statement.

I hope this information is helpful. If you have further
questions, please contact our staff.

Sincerely,


Ian Hamilton
Special Compliance Representative

MH:by

bcc: Jeanne P.

July 10, 1976


Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, California 95814

RE: ADVICE LETTER

Gentlemen:

I was a candidate for State Senate, 29th District. At the
end of the campaign, I found it necessary to borrow $20,000.00
to pay off campaign expenses. I called the bank to see about
arranging a loan and was advised it could be made at an interest
rate between 8½ and 9½. In the mean time a personal friend
heard about my plight and offered to loan me the money on a
five (5) year loan at 8½. It is lower than the bank but better
than the interest she could get in the bank. She does not
consider that this has anything to do with my campaign, just
a business transaction between two friends. She also does not
wish to have her name and address made public. She is a widow
and has already experienced one robbery. Now my question:
If I had borrowed from the bank, they would not have had to file
as a major contributor. I do not feel my lendor should have
to file either. As a republican - she did not even contribute
to my campaign. I have signed an Installment Note (interest
included) prepared by my attorney. I will pay interest only
for the months of July, August and September, and payments
of principal and interest begin on November 1, 1976. The
total time of the note 63 months.

I have received a informal opinion to the effect that she will
not have to file, but was advised to request a advice letter to
that I would have the opinion in writing. Please consider this
my request for Advice letter in the above mentioned matter.
Thank you,

Sincerely,

Kathryn L. Barr
10202 Bixby Avenue
Garden Grove, California 92640
Phone: (714) 539-5166

In the mean time a personal friend heard about my plight and offered to loan me the money on a five (5) year loan at 8%. Now my question: If I had borrowed from the bank, they would not have had to file as a major contributor. I do not feel my lender should have to file either.
